items.title: Conditional Independence Assumption
items.description: GNB multiplies per-feature Gaussian likelihoods because it assumes features are conditionally independent given the class. That assumption, when it holds or is close enough, makes the posterior tractable. Conditional Independence Assumption is the canonical home for the naive factorization — linked here, not re-derived.

items.title: Common Distributions (Gaussian / Normal)
items.description: Each class-conditional feature model is a Normal PDF N(x; μ, σ²). Common Distributions defines the Gaussian PDF, CDF, and when a Normal model is appropriate. This topic applies those PDFs as per-class per-feature likelihoods inside the Naive Bayes product.

items.title: k-Nearest Neighbors
items.description: Both are fast classical baselines for classification, but k-NN is instance-based (votes from nearby training points) while GNB is parametric-generative (fixed μ and σ² per class). k-NN needs no distributional assumption; GNB assumes Gaussian per-feature shapes and independence.

items.title: Logistic Regression
items.description: GNB models P(x|y) with Gaussian likelihoods then applies Bayes; logistic regression models P(y|x) directly via sigmoid. GNB can excel on small data with roughly Gaussian features; logistic regression makes fewer generative assumptions and learns a linear boundary in log-odds space.

items.title: Bayes' Theorem
items.description: GNB prediction is argmax_k P(y=k|x) via P(y=k)P(x|y=k). Bayes' Theorem is the canonical home for the posterior identity and evidence term; here we specialize P(x|y) to the naive Gaussian product.
